content.text: Any substance used to create an object is called a material. Common materials include paper, wood, metal, plastic, glass, and clay.

content.title: Identifying Materials
procedure: Observe objects around you and identify the materials they are made of.

content.title: Historical Context
content.text: Pottery in India dates back 7,000-8,000 years (Lahuradewa, Mehrgarh). The Harappan Civilization (2600-1900 BCE) perfected wheel-turned pottery with red surfaces and black designs. Baked clay is called 'terracotta'.
Classification is the method of arranging objects into groups based on common properties. This helps in studying patterns and finding items easily.

content.title: Grouping and Purpose
procedure: Group objects by shape, color, or material.
Observe how different balls bounce based on their material.
Think why a tumbler is made of glass/plastic and not cloth.
content.conclusion: Material choice depends on properties and the intended purpose of the object.

content.text: Lustre: Shiny surfaces, typically found in metals like gold, copper, and iron. Metals may look dull due to air/moisture.
Hardness: Hard materials are difficult to compress/scratch (e.g., stone, iron). Soft materials are easy to compress (e.g., sponge, rubber).

content.title: Feeling Materials
procedure: Touch objects to categorize them as hard or soft.

content.title: Categories of Transparency
items: Transparent: See clearly through (Glass, Water, Air).
Opaque: Cannot see through (Wood, Metals, Cardboard).
Translucent: See partially/hazy (Butter paper, Frosted glass).

content.title: Mixing in Water
procedure: STIR: Chalk powder, Sand, Sawdust (Insoluble - do not disappear).
content.conclusion: Materials that disappear are soluble; others are insoluble. Water dissolves many substances, which is vital for the human body and aquatic life (dissolved oxygen).

content.title: ORS (Oral Rehydration Solution)
content.text: Made by mixing 6 teaspoons of sugar and 1/2 teaspoon of salt in 1 litre of boiled-cooled water. Used to treat dehydration.
Matter is anything that occupies space and has mass. Mass is the quantity of matter. Volume is the space occupied by matter.

content.title: Measuring Mass
procedure: Weigh cups of water, sand, and pebbles to compare mass.

content.title: SI Rules
content.text: Always leave a space between number and unit (e.g., 5 kg). Abbreviate litre as capital L and millilitre as mL.
Ayurveda describes matter using 20 properties (gunas) arranged in 10 pairs of opposites. These describe physical matter, food, and living systems.

glossary.definition: A substance used to make an object.
The shiny appearance of a material's surface.
Anything that has mass and occupies space.
The process of grouping objects based on common properties.
Material through which objects can be seen but not clearly.
Capable of being dissolved in a liquid (like water).

summary_points: Objects are made of one or more materials chosen based on their properties.
Materials are classified by appearance (lustre), hardness, transparency, and solubility.
Anything with mass and volume is matter.
SI units include kg for mass and m³ for volume (often mL/L for liquids).
Ancient systems like Ayurveda classified matter using 20 opposite properties (gunas).
